channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Mechanical inputs boost diamond quantum sensor states as Q factor tops one million
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights how quantum sensing applications are racing ahead of quantum computing by leveraging diamond's exceptional properties for practical devices that work with far fewer qubits. While quantum computers struggle with the massive overhead of error correction requiring hundreds of thousands of qubits, diamond quantum sensors can deliver real-world functionality with minimal quantum resources, making them among the most commercially viable quantum technologies today. The achievement of a Q factor exceeding one million demonstrates remarkable coherence in these diamond-based systems, potentially enabling ultra-precise measurements for applications ranging from medical imaging to geological surveying. This work underscores why quantum sensing may be the first quantum technology to achieve widespread commercial deployment, paving the way for broader quantum adoption.

Most people think of diamonds as high-end adornments. Not Ania Bleszynski Jayich. The UC Santa Barbara physicist sees diamonds, which she grows in the UC Quantum Foundry, as a potentially powerful foundation for quantum sensors. Sensors are currently much farther along in their development than other potential quantum applications. Diamond sensors are particularly promising because diamonds require relatively few quantum bits (qubits) to operate, whereas a quantum computer, for instance, requires more than 100,000, perhaps as many as a million, qubits to handle error correction, one of the main hurdles for quantum computing.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Podcast with Mathematician Gil Kalai from Reichman University and the Hebrew University of Jerusalem
item.description:
Curator's Take: This podcast offers a valuable counterpoint to the prevailing optimism in quantum computing by featuring Gil Kalai, one of the field's most prominent skeptics who argues that correlated noise may fundamentally prevent effective quantum error correction. Kalai's mathematical perspective on why quantum supremacy claims like Google's 2019 demonstration might be flawed provides crucial intellectual balance in a field where technical challenges are often understated. His theories about complexity-based limitations on NISQ devices deserve serious consideration as the quantum computing community grapples with persistent noise and scaling challenges. While his predictions have proven controversial, engaging with such rigorous skepticism helps the field identify and address its most fundamental assumptions about achievable quantum advantage.

Yuval Boger interviews mathematician Gil Kalai about his long-standing skepticism regarding scalable quantum computing. Kalai explains two main arguments behind his theory: correlated noise that may defeat quantum error correction and complexity-based limits on NISQ devices achieving quantum supremacy. They discuss experimental claims such as Google’s 2019 result, potential tests of Kalai’s conjectures, and the [...] The post Podcast with Mathematician Gil Kalai from Reichman University and the Hebrew University of Jerusalem appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Toward Quantum Simulation of SU(2) Gauge Theory using Non-Compact Variables
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most ambitious applications: simulating the fundamental gauge theories that govern particle physics and the strong nuclear force. The team's orbifold lattice approach with non-compact variables represents a clever workaround to the notorious challenges of encoding gauge symmetries on quantum hardware, potentially reducing both qubit requirements and circuit complexity. What makes this particularly exciting is the validation through Monte Carlo benchmarking of SU(2) gauge theory in 2+1 dimensions, bringing us closer to quantum simulations that could reveal new physics beyond what classical computers can handle. These advances could eventually enable quantum computers to explore phenomena like quark confinement and the behavior of matter under extreme conditions found in neutron stars.

Simulating lattice gauge theories on quantum computers presents unique challenges that drive the development of novel theoretical frameworks. The orbifold lattice approach offers a scalable method for simulating SU($N$) gauge theories in arbitrary dimensions. In this work, we present three improvements: (i) two new simplified Hamiltonians, (ii) an encoding of the SU(2) theory with smaller number of qubits, and (iii) a reduction in the requirement for large scalar masses to reach the Kogut-Susskind limit, achieved via the inclusion of an additional term in the Hamiltonian. These advancements significantly reduce circuit depth and qubit requirements for quantum simulations. We benchmarked these improvements using Monte Carlo simulations of SU(2) in (2+1) dimensions. Preliminary results demonstrate the effectiveness of these developments and further validate the use of noncompact variables as a promising framework for scalable quantum simulations of gauge theories.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coexistence of CHSH Nonlocality and KCBS Contextuality in a Single Quantum State
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research reveals a fascinating tension at the heart of quantum mechanics by demonstrating that two fundamental quantum phenomena - contextuality and nonlocality - actually compete for resources within the same quantum state. The team's work with entangled qubit-qutrit systems shows that contextuality depends purely on population statistics while nonlocality requires quantum coherence, creating distinct parameter regimes where one phenomenon thrives while the other is suppressed. This separation of physical resources has profound implications for quantum technology design, as it suggests that devices optimized for contextuality-based quantum advantages may inherently struggle with nonlocality-based protocols, and vice versa. The discovery that these cornerstone quantum effects can only coexist in narrow parameter windows provides crucial guidance for engineers developing hybrid quantum systems that need to balance multiple quantum advantages.

Contextuality and nonlocality are distinct manifestations at the foundation of quantum mechanics, yet their coexistence within a single quantum state remains subtle. In a hybrid CHSH--KCBS scenario involving the entanglment of a qubit and a qutrit, the qutrit supports the KCBS contextuality test, and the CHSH nonlocality arises from correlations between the qubit and qutrit. Here, we derive the analytical closed-form expressions for both inequalities and also simulate this physics on a quantum circuit. We show that contextuality is governed solely by a population parameter $p_2$, associated with the occupation of the qutrit subsystem in the $|2\rangle$ level, which plays a distinguished role in the KCBS structure. In contrast, nonlocality depends irreducibly on coherence, involving both amplitudes and phases encoded in parameters $(X_i, Y_i)$. This separation of physical resources reveals parameter regimes that optimize KCBS violation while suppress CHSH violation, and vice versa. As a result, the optimal regions do not overlap, and coexistence is restricted to a narrow intermediate regime in parameter space.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: China’s Quantum Sector Sees Investment Surge as Larger Funding Rounds Return
item.description:
Curator's Take: China's quantum sector is experiencing a dramatic funding renaissance, with two startups securing nearly $145 million in venture capital within days of each other - a stark turnaround from the drought that followed when tech giants Alibaba and Baidu retreated from quantum investments. This surge coincides with Beijing's 15th Five-Year Plan officially designating quantum technology as the nation's top "future industry," signaling unprecedented government commitment to quantum leadership on the global stage. The timing is particularly significant as it comes during a period of intense international competition in quantum computing, with China clearly positioning itself to challenge Western dominance in this critical technology sector. These large funding rounds suggest that private investors are betting heavily on China's ability to translate government backing into commercial quantum breakthroughs, potentially accelerating the global quantum race.

As Beijing’s 15th Five-Year Plan designates quantum as the country’s top “future industry,” two startups close ~$145 million rounds within days of each other, marking the return of large-scale capital to a sector that went quiet after Alibaba and Baidu walked away. SpinQ Technology, a Shenzhen-based superconducting quantum computing company, completed a CNY 600 million ($87 million) Series C+ on April 3, bringing […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Chinese Quantum Startup QBoson Raises $145 Million to Scale Chip Production
item.description:
Curator's Take: This $145 million funding round for QBoson represents a significant milestone in China's aggressive push to commercialize quantum computing, moving beyond purely academic research toward industrial-scale manufacturing. The substantial investment underscores Beijing's strategic commitment to quantum supremacy and suggests Chinese companies are preparing to compete directly with Western firms like IBM, Google, and IonQ in the global quantum market. QBoson's focus on scaling chip production is particularly noteworthy, as manufacturing quantum processors at volume remains one of the field's biggest bottlenecks - if they can crack this challenge, it could dramatically accelerate quantum adoption across industries. This funding also signals that private investors are increasingly confident in quantum computing's near-term commercial viability, despite the technology's current limitations.

Insider Brief Chinese quantum startup QBoson has raised CNY 1 billion — or about $145 million USD — to expand chip production and scale its quantum computing systems, according to YiCai Global. The move reflects Beijing’s overall push to move the technology from lab experiments into industrial use. The Beijing-based company said it completed a […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Q-Factor Emerges from Stealth with $24M Seed Round to Scale Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals growing investor confidence in neutral atom quantum computing platforms, which offer unique advantages for scaling to large qubit counts compared to superconducting or trapped ion approaches. Q-Factor's ambitious million-qubit target represents one of the most aggressive scaling roadmaps announced by any quantum startup, potentially leapfrogging current leaders who are still working toward thousands of qubits. The substantial seed round and participation from major players like Intel Capital suggests the industry recognizes neutral atoms as a viable path to fault-tolerant quantum computing, especially given their natural identical properties and ability to be dynamically reconfigured. While a million qubits remains years away, Q-Factor's emergence adds another strong contender to the increasingly competitive race toward practical quantum advantage.

Q-Factor, a Tel Aviv-based quantum hardware startup, has exited stealth with $24 million (USD) in seed funding to develop a million-qubit neutral atom quantum computer. The investment round was led by NFX and TPY Capital, with significant participation from Intel Capital, Korea Investment Partners, Deep33, and the Matias family, alongside a grant from the Israel [...] The post Q-Factor Emerges from Stealth with $24M Seed Round to Scale Neutral Atom Systems appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Q-Factor Emerges From Stealth With $24 Million and Backing From Intel Capital
item.description:
Curator's Take: Q-Factor's emergence from stealth with $24 million in funding signals growing investor confidence in neutral atom quantum computing platforms, which offer compelling advantages like room-temperature operation and natural qubit connectivity. The backing from Intel Capital is particularly noteworthy, as it suggests the chip giant sees strategic value in this approach that could complement their existing quantum efforts with superconducting qubits. With neutral atoms gaining momentum through companies like QuEra and Pasqal, Q-Factor's Technion roots and substantial seed round position them as a serious contender in what's becoming an increasingly competitive quantum hardware landscape. The funding level also reflects the maturing quantum startup ecosystem, where proven teams can now raise significant capital to tackle the enormous technical challenges ahead.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Q-Factor, a neutral atom quantum computing company, today announced $24 million in seed funding. The round was led by NFX and TPY Capital, with participation from Intel Capital, Korea Investment Partners, Deep33, and the Matias family, along with a grant from the Israel Innovation Authority. The Technion – Israel Institute […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How Quantum Computing Affects Cryptography
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most pressing concerns in cybersecurity today: the looming threat that sufficiently powerful quantum computers pose to current encryption standards that protect everything from banking transactions to state secrets. While we're still years away from quantum computers capable of breaking RSA and elliptic curve cryptography at scale, the urgency stems from the "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks where adversaries collect encrypted data today with plans to crack it once quantum computers mature. The race to develop and standardize post-quantum cryptographic algorithms has become critical infrastructure planning, as organizations must begin transitioning their security systems well before quantum computers actually threaten existing encryption. This represents a fascinating intersection of cutting-edge physics and practical cybersecurity policy that will reshape how we protect digital information for decades to come.

Insider Brief The relationship between quantum computing and cryptography represents one of the most consequential technological races of the coming decade. On one side stands the promise of quantum computers powerful enough to break the encryption protecting global commerce, government secrets, and personal privacy. On the other stands the effort to develop and deploy new […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How Noise Limits The Size of Quantum Circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most fundamental challenges facing quantum computing today: how environmental noise places hard limits on the depth and complexity of quantum circuits we can reliably execute. The domino chain analogy effectively illustrates why even small errors compound exponentially as quantum circuits grow longer, making error correction and noise mitigation absolutely critical for scaling to practical quantum algorithms. Understanding these noise-induced limitations is essential for quantum hardware developers working to push beyond today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices toward fault-tolerant quantum computers. This represents the core engineering challenge that will determine whether quantum computing can transition from promising laboratory demonstrations to transformative real-world applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Imagine you’re trying to build a very long, complicated chain of dominoes. The aim is that each domino hits the next one perfectly, all the way down the line, producing an amazing result at the end. A quantum circuit is like a domino chain: A long chain of tiny steps […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Optimal, Qubit-Efficient Quantum Vehicle Routing via Colored-Permutations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most promising near-term applications for quantum computing - vehicle routing optimization - by developing a clever encoding scheme that dramatically reduces the number of qubits needed to represent the problem. The researchers' "colored-permutation" approach eliminates the need for explicit capacity tracking qubits, which has been a major bottleneck preventing quantum algorithms from scaling to realistic fleet sizes. What makes this particularly exciting is that they've demonstrated their method can find optimal solutions on standard benchmarks while using fewer quantum resources, bringing us closer to the crossover point where quantum computers might actually outperform classical methods on real logistics problems. The work represents exactly the kind of algorithmic innovation needed to squeeze maximum performance out of today's noisy, qubit-limited quantum processors.

We formulate a global-position colored-permutation encoding for the capacitated vehicle routing problem. Each of the $K$ vehicles selects a disjoint partial permutation, and the sum of these $K$ color layers forms a full $n\times n$ permutation matrix that assigns every customer to exactly one visit position. This representation uses $n^2K$ binary decision variables arranged as $K$ color layers over a common permutation structure, while vehicle capacities are enforced by weighted sums over the entries of each color class, requiring no explicit load register and hence no extra logical qubits beyond the routing variables. In contrast, many prior quantum encodings introduce an explicit capacity or load representation with additional qubits. Our construction is designed to exploit the Constraint-Enhanced QAOA framework together with its encoded-manifold analyses. Building on a requirements-based view of quantum utility in CVRP, we develop a routing optimization formulation that directly targets one of the main near-term bottlenecks, namely the additional logical-qubit cost of vehicle labels and explicit capacity constraints. Our proposal shows strong algorithmic performance in addition to qubit efficiency. On a standard benchmark suite, our end-to-end pipeline recovers the independently verified optima. The feasibility oracle may also be of independent interest as a reusable polynomial-time decoding and certification primitive for quantum and quantum-inspired routing pipelines.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Efficient direct quantum state tomography using fan-out couplings
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: efficiently verifying that quantum devices are actually producing the intended quantum states. The team's clever "fan-out coupling" approach allows them to measure specific elements of a quantum state's density matrix using constant circuit depth regardless of system size, which could dramatically reduce the exponential scaling problem that makes traditional quantum state tomography impractical for large systems. Their experimental demonstration on IBM hardware, successfully characterizing states up to 20 qubits with error mitigation, represents a significant step toward scalable quantum state verification that will be essential as quantum processors grow larger. This work could prove particularly valuable for validating quantum algorithms and debugging quantum hardware, where knowing exactly what quantum state you've created is crucial for progress.

Characterizing quantum states is essential for validating quantum devices, yet conventional quantum state tomography becomes prohibitively expensive as system size grows. Direct tomography offers a distinct route by enabling selective access to individual complex density-matrix elements, with a particular advantage for sparse target states and some verification tasks. Here we introduce a direct quantum state tomography scheme combining strong-measurement estimation with a fan-out coupling architecture. It enables mutually commuting interactions between system qubits and a single meter qubit, thereby achieving constant circuit depth, independent of system size. Notably, the involutory fan-out coupling reduces to the identity under repetition, enabling straightforward noise scaling for quantum error mitigation. We experimentally validate the scheme on a superconducting quantum processor via the IBM Quantum Platform, demonstrating four-qubit state reconstruction and single-circuit GHZ-state fidelity estimation up to 20 qubits with error mitigation. Consistent results with standard tomography and improved efficiency establish our scheme as a promising approach to reconstructing full quantum states and scalable verification tasks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Digital-Analog Quantum Simulation and Computing: A Perspective on Past and Future Developments
item.description:
Curator's Take: This perspective article highlights a promising middle path for quantum computing that could accelerate practical applications in the near term. Digital-analog quantum technologies cleverly combine the scalability advantages of analog quantum simulation with the versatility of digital gate-based computing, potentially offering a sweet spot between error accumulation and computational flexibility. This hybrid approach is particularly significant as it leverages the native interactions already present in quantum platforms, which could help bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and tomorrow's fault-tolerant quantum computers. The timing is especially relevant as the quantum computing field increasingly recognizes that different computational paradigms may be needed for different types of problems during the transition to full-scale quantum advantage.

Quantum simulation and computing traditionally has been based on two main paradigms, namely, digital and analog. In the digital paradigm, usually single and two-qubit gates (where qubit is an acronym for quantum bit) are employed as building blocks for scalable, universal quantum computing, although errors add up fast and error correction will be ultimately needed for scaling up. In the analog paradigm, large analog blocks are normally employed for a unitary dynamics that carries out the computation, enabling quantum operations on many qubits with reduced errors, but with the drawback of a limited choice of evolutions and lack of universality. In the past decade, a new paradigm has emerged, showing interesting possibilities for quantum simulation and computing in the near and mid term. This is the paradigm of digital-analog quantum technologies, which proposes to combine the best of both paradigms: large analog blocks, provided by native interactions of the employed quantum platform, enabling scalability, combined with digital gates, allowing for more versatility and, ultimately, universality. In this Perspective, I give an overview of the evolution of the field along the past decade, and an outlook for its future possibilities.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Clock Synchronization Networks: A Survey
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive survey arrives at a critical juncture as quantum networks evolve from laboratory demonstrations toward practical deployment, where precise time synchronization becomes essential for coordinating quantum operations across distributed nodes. The research highlights how quantum phenomena like entanglement and single-photon interference can potentially overcome fundamental limitations of classical timing methods, offering both enhanced precision and built-in security against spoofing attacks. As quantum internet infrastructure development accelerates, these quantum clock synchronization protocols could become foundational technology, much like how GPS timing underpins today's classical internet and financial systems. The survey's systematic categorization of different QCS approaches provides researchers and engineers with a crucial roadmap for selecting and implementing timing solutions that match the unique requirements of various quantum networking applications.

Quantum clock synchronization (QCS) aims to establish a shared temporal reference between distant nodes by exploiting uniquely quantum phenomena such as entanglement, single-photon interference, and quantum correlations. In contrast to classical synchronization and time-transfer techniques, which are limited by signal propagation delays, atmospheric disturbances, and oscillator drift, QCS protocols offer the potential to surpass classical precision bounds and enhance resilience against adversarial manipulations. As precise and secure time synchronization underpins distributed quantum networks, navigation systems, and emerging quantum Internet infrastructures, understanding QCS principles, capabilities, and implementation challenges has become increasingly important. This survey provides a unified and critical overview of the rapidly growing QCS research landscape, highlighting fundamentals, protocol types, enabling resources, performance constraints, security considerations, and practical implementations of QCS. We first introduce the theoretical underpinnings of QCS, including entanglement-assisted time transfer, Hong-Ou-Mandel interference-based synchronization, and quantum slow-clock transport. We then categorize the main QCS protocols, ranging from ticking-qubit and entanglement-based schemes to time-of-arrival correlation methods, conveyor-belt synchronization, and quantum-enhanced two-way time transfer. This organization clarifies the relationships between protocol families and their achievable precision advantages over classical methods. Key quantum resources such as spontaneous parametric down-conversion-based entangled photon pairs, Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger and W multipartite states, squeezed and frequency-entangled light, quantum frequency combs, and quantum memories are reviewed in the context of scalability and robustness.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Eliminating Vendor Lock-In in Quantum Machine Learning via Framework-Agnostic Neural Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most frustrating practical challenges in quantum machine learning today: the inability to move models between different quantum computing platforms without extensive rewrites. The proposed framework-agnostic architecture could be a game-changer for researchers who currently face the painful choice between committing to a single vendor ecosystem or maintaining multiple parallel implementations of their work. By creating a unified interface that works across IBM Quantum, Amazon Braket, Azure Quantum, and other major platforms, this approach could dramatically accelerate QML research by enabling true portability and reproducibility across the fragmented quantum computing landscape. If successful, this could help establish the kind of standardization that allowed classical machine learning to flourish across different hardware and software environments.

Quantum machine learning (QML) stands at the intersection of quantum computing and artificial intelligence, offering the potential to solve problems that remain intractable for classical methods. However, the current landscape of QML software frameworks suffers from severe fragmentation: models developed in TensorFlow Quantum cannot execute on PennyLane backends, circuits authored in Qiskit Machine Learning cannot be deployed to Amazon Braket hardware, and researchers who invest in one ecosystem face prohibitive switching costs when migrating to another. This vendor lock-in impedes reproducibility, limits hardware access, and slows the pace of scientific discovery. In this paper, we present a framework-agnostic quantum neural network (QNN) architecture that abstracts away vendor-specific interfaces through a unified computational graph, a hardware abstraction layer (HAL), and a multi-framework export pipeline. The core architecture supports simultaneous integration with TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX as classical co-processors, while the HAL provides transparent access to IBM Quantum, Amazon Braket, Azure Quantum, IonQ, and Rigetti backends through a single application programming interface (API). We introduce three pluggable data encoding strategies (amplitude, angle, and instantaneous quantum polynomial encoding) that are compatible with all supported backends. An export module leveraging Open Neural Network Exchange (ONNX) metadata enables lossless circuit translation across Qiskit, Cirq, PennyLane, and Braket representations. We benchmark our framework on the Iris, Wine, and MNIST-4 classification tasks, demonstrating training time parity (within 8\% overhead) compared to native framework implementations, while achieving identical classification accuracy.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The physical basis of information flow in neural matter: a thermocoherent perspective on cognitive dynamics
item.description:
Curator's Take: This intriguing paper ventures into the largely unexplored intersection of quantum coherence effects and neural information processing, proposing that biological brains might harness quantum correlations through what the authors call "thermocoherent" dynamics. The work suggests that various molecular structures in neural tissue - from ion channels to protein networks - could potentially support quantum information resources that operate beyond classical descriptions, offering a fresh perspective on how cognition might emerge from physical processes. While the connection between quantum mechanics and brain function remains highly speculative and contentious in neuroscience, this theoretical framework provides a rigorous resource-theoretic approach to studying information flow in biological systems. The research represents an ambitious attempt to bridge quantum information theory with neuroscience, though experimental validation of these quantum effects in warm, noisy biological environments would be essential to move from intriguing theory to established science.

Information flow is central to contemporary accounts of cognition, yet its physical basis in living neural matter remains poorly specified. Here, we develop a multiscale resource-theoretical framework motivated by the \textit{thermocoherent effect}, where heat flow is reciprocally coupled to a delocalized information flow carried by shared coherence and not reducible to local subsystem variables. Extending this line of work in light of recent results on correlation-enabled Mpemba-type thermal relaxation, we argue that the operational relevance of correlations depends less on their taxonomy than on their dynamical accessibility under the underlying interaction geometry. Relational structure encoded in the state of a single composite system -- including quantum entanglement, quantum discord, and classical correlations -- may therefore act as a usable physical resource that remains hidden from local subsystem descriptions. We propose that electrical, chemical, ionic, and thermal transport processes in neural matter may, under suitable microscopic conditions, generate or transduce partially hidden relational resources whose mutual coupling can progressively build larger-scale thermocoherent organization across spatial or spatiotemporal partitions in neural tissue. Ion-channel interfaces, hydrogen-bonded proton networks, aromatic $π$-electron architectures, and phosphate-rich motifs emerge as plausible substrate classes in which such resources may arise, become transiently accessible under environmental coupling, and leave coarse-grained signatures in neural dynamics. The resulting picture is neither a claim of macroscopic quantum cognition nor a reduction of cognition to abstract coding, but a falsifiable framework in which microscopic relational resources can bias transport, relaxation, signaling, and cross-scale neural coordination.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microstructural Topology as a Prescriptor for Quantum Coherence: Towards A Unified Framework for Decoherence in Superconducting Qubits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most persistent challenges in quantum computing: understanding exactly what causes superconducting qubits to lose their delicate quantum properties, known as decoherence. The authors propose a revolutionary framework that separates the microstructural characteristics of qubit materials from their geometric design, potentially allowing engineers to optimize each factor independently rather than through trial-and-error approaches that change multiple variables at once. What makes this particularly exciting is the introduction of "prescriptor classes" that could serve as universal design rules for building better qubits, along with standardized experimental protocols that would enable laboratories worldwide to share meaningful data. If validated, this framework could accelerate the development of more stable qubits by giving engineers precise, measurable targets for materials optimization rather than relying on empirical tweaking of fabrication processes.

In superconducting quantum circuits, decoherence improvements are frequently obtained through process interventions that simultaneously modify surface chemistry, microstructural topology, and device geometry, leaving mechanistic attribution structurally underdetermined. Predictive materials engineering requires measurable structural statistics to be separated from geometry-dependent coupling coefficients into independently testable factors. We introduce the concept of classical and quantum microstructure. In that context, we formulate a channel-wise separable framework for decoherence in superconducting transmon qubits in which each loss channel is described by a reduced prescriptor. Here, a channel-specific microstructural state variable is determined independently of device geometry, and a geometry-dependent coupling functional is computable from field solutions without reference to surface chemistry. We derive this product form from a spatially resolved kernel representation and establish a perturbative separability criterion that defines the regime where independent variation of the variables is valid. The framework specifies five prescriptor classes for dominant loss pathways in transmon-class devices. Falsifiability is operationalized through a pre-committed 2x2 experimental protocol in which the variables must satisfy independent ratio checks within propagated uncertainty. A Minimum-Dataset Specification standardizes reporting for cross-laboratory inference. Part I establishes the conceptual and mathematical architecture; coordinated experimental validation is reserved for Part II.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Osaka University and Fixstars Break 40-Qubit Barrier in Quantum Chemistry Simulation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates the impressive power of classical simulation techniques, with Osaka University and Fixstars pushing quantum chemistry simulations to 40 qubits using sophisticated classical computing methods. While this achievement highlights how classical computers can still tackle certain quantum problems at meaningful scales, it also serves as a crucial benchmark for quantum advantage research - helping define exactly where quantum computers need to perform to claim superiority over classical approaches. The work is particularly significant for quantum chemistry applications, where understanding molecular behavior at larger scales could accelerate drug discovery and materials science, regardless of whether the simulation runs on classical or quantum hardware. This classical milestone essentially raises the bar for quantum computers, making the eventual demonstration of quantum advantage in chemistry simulations even more meaningful when it occurs.

Large-scale classical simulation of IQPE quantum circuits demonstrated in this work. Larger qubit counts and more Hamiltonian terms result in deeper circuits and longer simulation times. Credit: QIQB, The University of Osaka Osaka University’s Center for Quantum Information and Quantum Biology (QIQB) and Fixstars Corporation have established a new benchmark in classical quantum simulation, utilizing [...] The post Osaka University and Fixstars Break 40-Qubit Barrier in Quantum Chemistry Simulation appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Rigetti Sells Novera QPU to University of Saskatchewan for Canada’s First Open-Architecture System
item.description:
Curator's Take: This sale represents a significant shift toward democratizing quantum computing access through on-premises university installations, marking Canada's entry into the growing trend of academic institutions owning their own quantum systems rather than relying solely on cloud access. The open-architecture design of Rigetti's Novera QPU is particularly noteworthy as it allows researchers to experiment with the full quantum stack, from hardware control to algorithm development, fostering deeper understanding and innovation that's often constrained in cloud-only environments. This milestone reflects the maturing quantum hardware market, where companies like Rigetti are finding sustainable business models by selling smaller, more accessible systems to educational institutions that can serve as training grounds for the next generation of quantum researchers and engineers.

Rigetti Computing (Nasdaq: RGTI) has officially expanded its on-premises footprint with the sale of a 9-qubit Novera™ QPU to the University of Saskatchewan (USask). This acquisition marks a historic milestone as USask becomes home to the first university-owned and operated, vendor-supported, full-stack, open-architecture quantum computer in Canada. The system is housed within USask’s Centre for [...] The post Rigetti Sells Novera QPU to University of Saskatchewan for Canada’s First Open-Architecture System appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Infleqtion and Safran Launch Integrated Quantum Timing Solution for GNSS-Challenged Environments
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step toward commercializing quantum sensing technology for real-world applications, particularly in environments where GPS signals are compromised or unavailable. Infleqtion's quantum optical clock technology addresses a critical vulnerability in modern infrastructure that relies heavily on GPS for precise timing synchronization, from financial trading systems to power grids. The collaboration with Safran, an established aerospace and defense company, provides the integration expertise and market channels needed to deploy quantum timing solutions at scale. This marks an important milestone in the transition of quantum sensing from laboratory curiosities to practical tools that can enhance national security and critical infrastructure resilience.

Infleqtion (NYSE: INFQ), a specialist in neutral-atom quantum technology, and Safran Electronics & Defense have announced the commercial availability of a quantum-enabled precision timing solution. The system integrates Infleqtion’s Tiqker™ quantum optical clock with Safran’s established White Rabbit and SecureSync® synchronization platforms. Aimed at defense, telecommunications, and critical infrastructure sectors, the solution is designed to [...] The post Infleqtion and Safran Launch Integrated Quantum Timing Solution for GNSS-Challenged Environments appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: GPU-Accelerated Quantum Simulation: Empirical Backend Selection, Gate Fusion, and Adaptive Precision
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical bottleneck in quantum computing development: the exponential computational cost of classically simulating quantum circuits, which remains essential for debugging algorithms and validating hardware in today's NISQ devices. The researchers' GPU-accelerated framework cleverly addresses real-world engineering challenges by automatically selecting the best computational backend at runtime and implementing smart optimizations like gate fusion and adaptive precision that can deliver 64x to 146x speedups over traditional CPU simulation. What makes this particularly valuable is the practical focus on memory management and seamless integration with popular quantum software platforms like Qiskit and Cirq, suggesting this could become a widely-adopted tool for quantum developers. While classical simulation will always hit fundamental limits, optimizations like these extend the range of quantum circuits we can efficiently test and validate, accelerating the entire field's progress toward practical quantum applications.

Classical simulation of quantum circuits remains indispensable for algorithm development, hardware validation, and error analysis in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era. However, state-vector simulation faces exponential memory scaling, with an n-qubit system requiring O(2^n) complex amplitudes, and existing simulators often lack the flexibility to exploit heterogeneous computing resources at runtime. This paper presents a GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulation framework that introduces three contributions: (1) an empirical backend selection algorithm that benchmarks CuPy, PyTorch-CUDA, and NumPy-CPU backends at runtime and selects the optimal execution path based on measured throughput; (2) a directed acyclic graph (DAG) based gate fusion engine that reduces circuit depth through automated identification of fusible gate sequences, coupled with adaptive precision switching between complex64 and complex128 representations; and (3) a memory-aware fallback mechanism that monitors GPU memory consumption and gracefully degrades to CPU execution when resources are exhausted. The framework integrates with Qiskit, Cirq, PennyLane, and Amazon Braket through a unified adapter layer. Benchmarks on an NVIDIA A100-SXM4 (40 GiB) GPU demonstrate speedups of 64x to 146x over NumPy CPU execution for state-vector simulation of circuits with 20 to 28 qubits, with speedups exceeding 5x from 16 qubits onward. Hardware validation on an IBM quantum processing unit (QPU) confirms Bell state fidelity of 0.939, a five-qubit Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) state fidelity of 0.853, and circuit depth reduction from 42 to 14 gates through the fusion pipeline. The system is designed for portability across NVIDIA consumer and data-center GPUs, requiring no vendor-specific compilation steps.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: How Events Separated by a Timelike Interval Can Help Us Understand Quantum Nonlocality
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum mechanics' most enduring puzzles by exploring how events separated in time might illuminate the mysterious nature of quantum nonlocality in EPR correlations. While Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen entanglement remains central to quantum cryptography and computing applications, the fundamental interpretation of these "spooky" correlations continues to divide physicists nearly a century after the original thought experiment. The authors' approach of using timelike-separated events to understand spacelike quantum correlations represents an intriguing theoretical angle that could potentially bridge different interpretations of quantum nonlocality. Though this appears to be foundational physics rather than immediate practical advancement, clearer understanding of quantum nonlocality's true nature could eventually inform more robust quantum cryptographic protocols and deepen our grasp of entanglement's role in quantum computing.

Quantum entanglement plays a fundamental role in quantum cryptography and computation. An important example of quantum entanglement can be found in the correlations of Einstein, Podolsky, and Rosen (EPR). However, despite the plethora of articles related to the topic, different interpretations of the EPR correlations coexist, and a consensus has not yet been reached. In this article, we seek to demonstrate, through the simple and direct application of quantum formalism, how events separated by timelike intervals can, strangely enough, help us better understand some aspects of the so-called "quantum nonlocality" associated with EPR correlations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Is There a Silver Lining Behind The Looming Dark Clouds of Quantum’s Crypto-busting Powers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial shift in perspective around Google's recent quantum cryptography research, moving beyond the doom-and-gloom narrative about breaking current encryption to focus on the positive implications for near-term quantum applications. The same quantum advances that pose risks to cryptographic security are simultaneously demonstrating that practical quantum computing may be closer than many experts previously anticipated, potentially accelerating breakthroughs in optimization, simulation, and other commercially valuable use cases. This represents an important reframing of the quantum timeline debate, suggesting that the path to quantum advantage across multiple domains may be more compressed than the traditional decades-long projections that have dominated industry discourse. The research underscores how quantum computing's disruptive potential cuts both ways, creating both challenges and unprecedented opportunities in roughly the same timeframe.

Insider Brief The alarm over Google Quantum AI’s latest findings has centered on risk, but the same advance is sharpening the case that useful quantum applications may arrive sooner than expected. A recent white paper written by a Google-led research team outlined how a sufficiently large quantum system — on the order of hundreds of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Using Digital Twin Methods to Accurately Simulate Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a significant step toward practical quantum error correction by applying digital twin methodology - creating precise virtual replicas of quantum hardware - to accurately model real-world imperfections like crosstalk between qubits. The team's simulation of a distance-7 surface code with 97 qubits demonstrates how digital twins can capture the complex interactions that cause errors in actual quantum processors, moving beyond idealized theoretical models. This approach could accelerate the development of fault-tolerant quantum computers by allowing researchers to test and optimize error correction protocols in realistic virtual environments before implementing them on expensive quantum hardware. The ability to precisely model crosstalk patterns and other hardware-specific noise sources marks a crucial bridge between the theoretical promise of quantum error correction and its practical implementation.

Diagram of a distance‑7 rotated surface code with 97 physical qubits (49 data qubits + 48 measurement qubits) modelled by the team. Grey nodes are data qubits; labeled circles are ancillas measuring X- or Z-type checks on neighboring data qubits. Edge colors encode the residual ZZ crosstalk strength (darker edges indicate stronger crosstalk).Credit: Quantum Elements [...] The post Using Digital Twin Methods to Accurately Simulate Quantum Error Correction appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Palm Beach State College Takes Aim at Quantum Workforce Demand
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a critical bottleneck that could determine quantum computing's real-world success: the massive shortage of skilled technicians and practitioners needed to operate, maintain, and support quantum systems as they transition from research labs to commercial deployment. While much attention focuses on quantum algorithms and hardware breakthroughs, Palm Beach State College's initiative addresses the equally important challenge of building a workforce pipeline that extends beyond PhD researchers to include community college-trained technicians who can handle the practical aspects of quantum technology implementation. This workforce development approach mirrors how other high-tech industries scaled successfully, recognizing that widespread adoption requires accessible training programs that can quickly produce the thousands of skilled workers needed to support quantum infrastructure across industries.

Insider Brief Quantum technology is often portrayed as a field staffed by people with doctoral degrees and years of academic experience. That may be how it is now, but experts are increasingly concerned that as quantum moves out of theory and into practice and out of the laboratory and into the real world, its success […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SpinQ Technology Secures Nearly 1 Billion Chinese Yuan in Series C Funding to Scale Industrial Superconducting Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This significant funding milestone positions SpinQ as a major player in the global race to commercialize quantum computing, particularly noteworthy as China continues to heavily invest in quantum technologies as a strategic priority. The focus on "industrial superconducting quantum computing" suggests SpinQ is targeting practical enterprise applications rather than just research demonstrations, which could accelerate real-world quantum adoption. With nearly $139 million in total Series C funding, SpinQ joins the ranks of well-capitalized quantum companies like IonQ and Rigetti, indicating that the quantum computing market is maturing beyond pure venture capital into serious industrial investment. This funding level also reflects the growing confidence that quantum computing is transitioning from laboratory curiosity to commercially viable technology within the next decade.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SpinQ Technology, a leading global provider of full-stack quantum computing solutions, today announced the successful completion of its 600 million Chinese Yuan (about $83 million U.S.) Series C+ financing round. This milestone brings the company’s total Series C funding to nearly 1 billion Chinese Yuan (about $139 million U.S.) within […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Coinbase CEO Addresses ‘Quantum Threat’ to Cryptocurrencies
item.description:
Curator's Take: This marks a significant shift in the cryptocurrency world's approach to quantum computing, moving from dismissive skepticism to proactive preparation as quantum error correction milestones continue to accelerate. Armstrong's decision to personally spearhead Bitcoin's quantum-resistance efforts reflects growing industry recognition that practical quantum computers capable of breaking current cryptographic standards may arrive sooner than previously expected. The timing is particularly noteworthy given recent breakthroughs from companies like Google and IBM in quantum error correction, which have brought fault-tolerant quantum computing closer to reality. This represents one of the first major real-world industries beginning serious quantum-safe migration planning, potentially serving as a bellwether for broader cybersecurity preparedness across critical infrastructure.

Insider Brief A steady stream of announcements that quantum teams are making progress on quantum error correction seems to have stirred the once-skeptical cryptocurrency community. Coinbase CEO and co-founder Brian Armstrong said he will personally lead an industry push to make Bitcoin quantum-resistant, marking a shift from long-term concern to near-term engineering priority after new […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuEra Launches Open-Source Package to Simulate Logical Quantum Circuits at Scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This release represents a crucial step forward in quantum error correction development, as simulating non-Clifford operations like T-gates has traditionally been one of the most computationally expensive bottlenecks in QEC research. QuEra's GPU-accelerated simulator addresses a fundamental challenge that has limited researchers' ability to test and optimize error correction protocols at realistic scales. By open-sourcing this tool, QuEra is democratizing access to advanced QEC simulation capabilities that were previously available only to well-resourced research groups, potentially accelerating the entire field's progress toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. The timing is particularly significant as the quantum computing community increasingly focuses on demonstrating logical qubits and practical error correction schemes.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuEra Computing today open-sourced its T-gate simulator (Tsim), a GPU-accelerated quantum circuit simulator that, for the first time, lets researchers simulate non-Clifford gate operations at the speed and scale that quantum error correction (QEC) development demands. QEC is the essential bridge between today’s noisy quantum processors and the fault-tolerant machines […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum entanglement between electrons and ions captured at attosecond timescale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a significant leap in our ability to observe and measure quantum entanglement in real-time, capturing these mysterious correlations between electrons and ions at the attosecond scale - that's quintillionths of a second. The achievement is particularly exciting because it moves beyond theoretical descriptions of entanglement to direct experimental observation of how these quantum correlations form and evolve at the fundamental timescales of atomic processes. This level of precision in measuring entanglement dynamics could prove crucial for developing more robust quantum computing systems, where understanding and controlling entanglement decoherence is essential for maintaining quantum information. The work also opens new possibilities for studying how quantum effects transition to classical behavior, potentially informing the design of quantum technologies that can better preserve entangled states.

Quantum mechanics is extremely successful at describing the behavior of matter at the atomic level. This success forces one to accept that certain aspects of physical reality go far beyond our intuition. Among these, none is more intriguing than the concept of quantum entanglement, which mathematically describes how two particles that have at some point in the past interacted with each other retain a memory of this interaction to such an extent that acting on one of the two particles has a measurable influence on the properties of the other particle, even if the two have long ago stopped interacting and may be separated by such a vast distance that communication between them is no longer possible.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Decoding Realistic Quantum Error Syndrome with Quantum Elements Digital Twins
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most critical engineering challenges: determining the precise hardware quality and code size needed to achieve practical fault-tolerant quantum computing. AWS's digital twin approach represents a significant step toward bridging the gap between theoretical error correction thresholds and real-world implementation, where factors like crosstalk, noise correlations, and hardware imperfections create complex syndrome patterns that ideal models miss. By simulating realistic error syndromes, this work could accelerate the timeline for useful quantum advantage by helping engineers optimize both hardware design and error correction protocols simultaneously. This type of practical engineering analysis is exactly what the field needs as we move from proof-of-concept demonstrations to building quantum computers that can solve real problems.

Fault-tolerant quantum computing requires quantum error correction (QEC): Encoding one logical qubit into many physical qubits so that, below a threshold error rate, the logical error rate falls rapidly as the code grows. The practical engineering question is: How large must the code be and how good must the hardware be to reach a useful […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Novel approach to quantum error correction portends a scalable future for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: the massive overhead required for error correction, where thousands of physical qubits are typically needed to create a single reliable logical qubit. Dr. Williamson's "gauging logical operators" approach promises to dramatically reduce this burden, potentially bringing practical fault-tolerant quantum computers closer to reality by making them more resource-efficient. The work represents a crucial theoretical breakthrough in error correction schemes, as reducing qubit overhead is essential for scaling beyond today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices to the large-scale systems needed for transformative applications like drug discovery and cryptography. Published in Nature Physics, this advance could accelerate the timeline for when quantum computers become commercially viable for solving real-world problems.

A University of Sydney quantum physicist has developed a new approach to quantum error correction that could significantly reduce the number of physical qubits required to build large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers. The study, co-authored by Dr. Dominic Williamson from the School of Physics, is titled "Low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computation by gauging logical operators" and published in Nature Physics.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computing deployment, as trapped ion systems have traditionally required room-sized laboratory setups with complex laser and vacuum systems. Making this technology portable could accelerate the transition from research labs to real-world applications, potentially enabling quantum sensors for navigation, geological surveying, and field-based scientific measurements. The miniaturization also suggests that trapped ion quantum computers might eventually compete with other platforms in terms of accessibility and deployment flexibility. While the article summary is brief, any significant reduction in the size and complexity of trapped ion systems would be a notable engineering achievement that brings quantum technologies closer to everyday use.

Portable device could find applications in quantum computing and optical clocks The post Trapped ion quantum technology gets smaller appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Useful quantum computers could be built with as few as 10,000 qubits, team finds
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents a potential game-changer for the quantum computing timeline, as reducing the qubit requirements by two orders of magnitude could dramatically accelerate when we see practical quantum computers. The collaboration between Caltech theorists and the startup Oratomic suggests they've found innovative error correction techniques that work more efficiently than previously thought possible, addressing one of quantum computing's biggest hurdles. While current quantum computers from IBM, Google, and others are approaching the 1,000-qubit range, this finding indicates that truly useful quantum advantage might be achievable with systems containing 10,000-20,000 qubits rather than the millions once thought necessary. If validated, this could push practical quantum computing applications from the 2030s into the late 2020s, making quantum breakthroughs in drug discovery, cryptography, and optimization much more imminent.

Quantum computers of the future may be closer to reality thanks to new research from Caltech and Oratomic, a Caltech-linked start-up company. Theorists and experimentalists teamed up to develop a new approach for reducing the errors that riddle today's rudimentary quantum computers. Whereas these machines were previously thought to require millions of qubits to work properly (qubits being the quantum equivalent to 1's and 0's in classical computers), the new results indicate that a fully realized quantum computer could be built with as few as 10,000 to 20,000 qubits. The need for fewer qubits means that quantum computers could, in theory, be operational by the end of the decade.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Silicon quantum computer performs logical operations for the first time
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough marks a pivotal moment for quantum computing by demonstrating that silicon - the backbone of classical computing - can successfully perform logical quantum operations, bridging the gap between today's semiconductor industry and tomorrow's quantum processors. The achievement is particularly significant because logical qubits, which use error correction to maintain quantum information, are essential for building practical quantum computers that can run useful algorithms reliably. Silicon's natural compatibility with existing chip fabrication infrastructure means this advance could accelerate the path to manufacturing quantum processors at scale, potentially leveraging decades of semiconductor manufacturing expertise. This represents a major step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing using a platform that the tech industry already knows how to produce commercially.

Silicon is ubiquitous in modern electronics, and now it is becoming increasingly useful in quantum computing. In particular, silicon's compatibility with existing chip technology and its long coherence times in silicon-based spin qubits make it a promising material for scalable quantum computing. A new study, published in Nature Nanotechnology, has demonstrated silicon's use in a logical quantum processor, representing the first of its kind.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion
item.description:
Curator's Take: Google's decision to pursue both superconducting and neutral atom quantum computing platforms represents a significant strategic pivot that acknowledges the fundamental uncertainties still facing the field. This dual-modality approach hedges against the possibility that superconducting qubits alone may not achieve the scale and coherence needed for practical quantum advantage, while neutral atoms offer compelling advantages in connectivity and potential for larger qubit counts. The move follows similar multi-platform strategies from other major players like Amazon and IBM, suggesting the industry is maturing beyond single-technology bets toward more diversified research portfolios. This expansion could accelerate progress by allowing Google to leverage the unique strengths of each platform while cross-pollinating insights between their hardware teams.

Google Quantum AI has announced a major strategic expansion of its research roadmap, adding neutral atom quantum computing to its established superconducting hardware program. Led by founder Hartmut Neven, the move signals a shift toward a multi-platform approach to reach commercially relevant quantum advantage. While Google remains confident in the timeline for superconducting systems by [...] The post Google Quantum AI Adopts Dual-Modality Strategy with Neutral Atom Expansion appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: New Zealand Researchers Report Advance in Optical Ising Machine for Hybrid Quantum Optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research highlights an important trend in quantum computing where hybrid classical-quantum systems are emerging as practical stepping stones toward full-scale quantum advantage. Optical Ising machines represent a particularly promising approach because they can leverage quantum-inspired physics to tackle complex optimization problems without requiring the extreme engineering challenges of fault-tolerant quantum computers. The New Zealand team's focus on scalability is especially significant, as many current quantum and quantum-inspired systems struggle to maintain performance advantages as problem sizes grow. While we await the arrival of fully fault-tolerant quantum computers, these hybrid optimization devices could deliver real-world value in logistics, finance, and materials science much sooner than traditional quantum computing timelines suggest.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — With the world’s first scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computer expected to be years away, a group of New Zealand researchers has developed an exciting hybrid device that could solve big, intractable problems sooner. Research Fellow, Dr Liam Quinn of Te Whai Ao — Dodd-Walls Centre is leading the work, considered of […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Washington Begins to Treat Quantum Technology as a Strategic Priority
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a crucial shift as quantum technology moves from research curiosity to national security imperative in Washington's policy circles. The bipartisan engagement suggests lawmakers are beginning to understand quantum's dual nature as both an enormous economic opportunity and a potential threat to current cryptographic systems that underpin everything from financial transactions to government communications. What makes this particularly significant is the timing - as quantum computers edge closer to practical applications, the window for establishing regulatory frameworks, funding priorities, and international cooperation agreements is narrowing rapidly. The fact that these discussions are happening now, rather than after quantum advantage becomes widespread, suggests policymakers may finally be getting ahead of the technology curve for once.

Insider Brief Tuesday in Washington offered a clear picture of where quantum technology now stands, not as a future concept, but as a present responsibility. Meetings on Capitol Hill, organized by Optica, brought together lawmakers, federal agencies, and leaders from across the quantum ecosystem to focus on what comes next. The conversations were not about […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Paves a Two-Lane Quantum Roadmap by Adding Neutral Atom Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals a major strategic shift for Google Quantum AI, which has built its quantum supremacy claims and current leadership position around superconducting qubits but is now hedging its bets by exploring neutral atom systems. The move reflects growing industry recognition that different quantum hardware approaches may excel at different types of problems, with neutral atoms potentially offering advantages in connectivity and scalability that could complement Google's superconducting expertise. By diversifying its quantum hardware portfolio, Google is positioning itself to capitalize on whichever technology proves most viable for near-term commercial applications, while also acknowledging that the path to fault-tolerant quantum computing may require multiple parallel approaches rather than betting everything on a single platform.

Insider Brief Google Quantum AI, which was a pioneer in the superconducting approach to quantum computing, announced that it is expanding beyond that core approach to add neutral atom quantum computing, a move the company says will accelerate progress toward commercially useful machines. The plan, discussed in a recent Google Quantum AI blog post, would […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Scalable quantum circuit generation for iterative ground state approximation using Majorana Propagation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work tackles one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications - finding ground states of molecular systems - by cleverly combining classical preprocessing with quantum circuit design. The researchers demonstrate their ADAPT-VMPE algorithm can generate practical quantum circuits for systems as large as 100 qubits while maintaining polynomial scaling, which is crucial for making quantum chemistry simulations feasible on near-term devices. What makes this particularly exciting is their focus on a real-world photosensitizer molecule used in cancer treatment, showing how quantum algorithms could eventually accelerate drug discovery and materials science. The theoretical guarantees on approximation error and the polynomial complexity bounds suggest this approach could bridge the gap between today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices and useful quantum advantage in molecular simulation.

We introduce the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter ansatz Variational Majorana Propagation Eigensolver (ADAPT-VMPE), a quantum-inspired classical algorithm that exploits Majorana Propagation (MP) to produce circuits for approximating the ground state of molecular Hamiltonians. Equipped with the theoretical guarantees of MP, which provide controllable bounds on the approximation error, ADAPT-VMPE offers an efficient and scalable approach for iterative ansatz construction. A theoretical analysis of the computational complexity demonstrates that it is polynomial in both the number of qubits and the number of iterations. We present an in-depth analysis of circuit construction strategies, analyzing their impact on convergence and provide practical guidance for efficient ansatz generation. Using ADAPT-VMPE, we construct up to 100-qubit ansätze for a strongly correlated photosensitizer currently undergoing human clinical trials for cancer treatment. Our results demonstrate that constant overlap with the ground state across system sizes can be reached in polynomial time with polynomially sized circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough pathway for experimentally realizing Motzkin spin chains, exotic quantum systems that have tantalized theorists for years due to their unusual entanglement properties that violate fundamental scaling laws. Unlike typical quantum systems where entanglement grows with the boundary area between regions, Motzkin chains exhibit logarithmic scaling that makes them notoriously difficult to simulate on classical computers, yet they hold deep connections to topological phases and even string theory through AdS/CFT correspondence. The researchers' proposed Rydberg atom platform offers a realistic experimental route to create these mathematical constructs in the lab, potentially opening doors to studying a whole new class of highly entangled quantum phases that could inform both fundamental physics and future quantum technologies. This work exemplifies how programmable quantum simulators are becoming powerful tools for exploring exotic quantum matter that exists at the edge of what's computationally tractable.

Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: NRFC Investment Accelerates SQC’s Atomic-Scale Manufacturing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Silicon Quantum Computing's unique atomic-scale manufacturing approach, which positions them as the sole company worldwide capable of fabricating quantum chips with atomic precision. The AUD$20 million from Australia's National Reconstruction Fund Corporation not only validates SQC's silicon-based quantum technology but also strengthens Australia's position in the global quantum race, particularly in the critical hardware manufacturing sector where precision at the atomic level remains one of the field's greatest technical challenges. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's claim of already delivering quantum-enhanced AI products to enterprise and government clients today, suggesting their technology has moved beyond pure research into practical applications. This combination of manufacturing precision and commercial readiness could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum computing deployment, especially in applications where silicon-based quantum systems offer advantages over other qubit technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) has invested AUD$20 million in Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC), a global leader in the field of quantum computing. SQC is the only company in the world capable of manufacturing quantum chips with atomic precision while delivering quantum enhanced-AI products for enterprises and governments today. It is […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Global control via quantum actuators
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces an elegant solution to one of quantum computing's biggest architectural challenges: how to perform selective quantum operations without needing individual control over every qubit. The proposed quantum actuators act like smart intermediaries that can temporarily store and release energy on command, allowing processors to activate specific multi-qubit gates even when using simple global control fields that affect all qubits simultaneously. This approach could dramatically simplify the control electronics needed for large-scale quantum computers while actually enhancing their connectivity and enabling long-range quantum operations between distant qubits. The connection to quantum thermodynamics through the "quantum battery" interpretation adds theoretical depth and suggests these actuators might serve dual purposes in future quantum processors.

We introduce the concept of quantum actuators as mediators for globally controlled quantum computation. Auxiliary quantum systems act as controllable elements that transiently store and release interaction energy, enabling the selective activation of multi-qubit gates within globally driven architectures. During compilation they remain passive and require no fine-grained local control, while during operation they allow for controlled activation of interactions and directional flow of quantum information. We provide a framework for embedding quantum actuators in globally controlled processors, showing how they enhance connectivity, enable long-range entangling operations, and bridge distant regions without increasing local control overhead. We discuss physical implementations and architectural strategies illustrating how these elements extend the capabilities of global-control schemes. A complementary interpretation in terms of quantum batteries naturally emerges, connecting global-control architectures with concepts from quantum thermodynamics while highlighting the distinct operational role of quantum actuators.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Lawmaker Proposes U.K.-U.S. Quantum Partnership Can be a Strategic Counterweight to China
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights how quantum computing is increasingly viewed through the lens of national security and international competition, with policymakers recognizing that quantum supremacy could reshape global power dynamics. Tugendhat's proposal for deeper U.K.-U.S. quantum collaboration reflects growing concerns about China's substantial investments in quantum research and the strategic importance of maintaining Western leadership in this critical technology. The mention of Quantinuum as a model partnership underscores how the quantum industry is already operating across borders, suggesting that formal government partnerships could amplify existing commercial and research ties. This signals a shift from viewing quantum computing as purely a scientific endeavor to recognizing it as a cornerstone of future economic and military competitiveness.

Insider Brief A transatlantic partnership between the U.S. and the U.K. could serve as a fulcrum in the global effort to develop quantum computing, with Quantinuum positioned as a leading example of how combined capabilities may accelerate progress and shape geopolitical competition. In a Wall Street Journal opinion column, Tom Tugendhat, a member of the […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Study Finds More Qubits Yield Better Financial Optimization Outcomes
item.description:
